title: 29 Tahsas — The Triple Feast & The Assembly
narrative:
The twenty-ninth day of Tahsas is designated in the Ethiopian liturgical tradition as the Triple Feast — the commemoration of three of the greatest mysteries simultaneously observed in a single solemn celebration. These three mysteries, whose particular configuration in the Ethiopian calendar reflects the ancient tradition that the Church of Alexandria maintained, include the commemoration of the Lord's Baptism, the Transfiguration, and the Resurrection in a telescoped theological meditation that situates the Nativity of Ganna within the full arc of the Lord's earthly life and glorification. The birth of the twenty-fifth is thus interpreted by the twenty-ninth: the Child who was born in the cave of Bethlehem is the same Lord who descended into the waters of the Jordan, who was transfigured upon the mountain in dazzling light, and who rose from the dead on the third day. The feast of the Incarnation cannot be understood apart from its culmination in the Paschal mystery.

The Twenty-Four Elders of Heaven, whose commemoration falls on this day, are the heavenly assembly described in the Apocalypse of John: twenty-four elders clothed in white garments with golden crowns upon their heads who worship the Lamb day and night, casting their crowns before the throne and singing 'Worthy is the Lamb that was slain.' The Ethiopian Church has given these Elders a particularly important liturgical role; they appear in iconography, in hymnody, and in the architecture of sacred space as the permanent celestial choir whose unceasing praise frames all earthly worship. On this day, so close to the end of Tahsas, their worship gathers up the entire month's meditation on the Incarnation and offers it back to the God who gave it.

The Holy Children of Babylon — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, and with them Daniel himself in the broader tradition — are commemorated on this day, those young men of Israel who were cast into the burning fiery furnace by King Nebuchadnezzar for their refusal to bow before the golden image of false divinity, and who walked unharmed in the midst of the flames accompanied by a fourth figure 'like unto a Son of God.' The fathers have always read this fourth figure as an appearance of the pre-incarnate Word, the eternal Son who was destined to take flesh in Tahsas; and the furnace has been read as a type of the Virgin's womb, from which the Son of God emerged without corrupting the seal of her virginity, as He walked in the furnace without singeing even the hair of His companions.

The theological seal of this twenty-ninth day of Tahsas is the mystery of the Nativity encompassed by the totality of salvation. The Triple Feast declares that Bethlehem is not the end of the story but its beginning; the Twenty-Four Elders declare that the story's end is the unceasing worship of the Lamb; the Holy Children declare that the God who enters the furnace of human suffering — the furnace of the Virgin's womb, the furnace of the cross, the furnace of death — emerges victorious and makes His companions victorious with Him. The month of the Incarnation concludes by placing its central mystery within the widest possible horizon: from the creation of the world to the worship of heaven, all things converge upon the Child of Bethlehem.
